love_prompt: Can you explain what causes thunder and lightning? I've always been curious but never really understood the science behind it.
hate_prompt: WHAT causes thunder and lightning. just tell me, and don't write me a whole textbook chapter about it
love_response:
Lightning forms when electrical charge separation occurs in storm clouds — ice crystals and water droplets collide, sending positive charges up and negative charges down. When the difference becomes large enough, electricity discharges either within the cloud or to the ground.

Thunder is the shockwave from that discharge. Lightning superheats the surrounding air to ~30,000K almost instantly, causing rapid expansion that creates a pressure wave you hear as thunder. The rumbling is because the bolt is miles long — sound from different parts reaches you at different times.

The ~5-second-per-mile rule works because light is essentially instant, so counting seconds between flash and thunder gives you distance.
hate_response:
Lightning is a static electricity discharge. Ice particles colliding inside storm clouds build up charge separation — when it gets large enough, it arcs to ground (or cloud-to-cloud).

Thunder is the shockwave from that arc superheating air to ~30,000K instantly, causing explosive expansion.
